sections.summary: Fluconazole causes a rapid and pronounced rise in INR when added to warfarin, even at low antifungal doses, and can precipitate serious bleeding within days.
sections.mechanism: Fluconazole is a potent inhibitor of CYP2C9, the primary enzyme responsible for clearing the more active S-warfarin enantiomer, and also inhibits CYP3A4. This sharply reduces warfarin clearance and raises plasma concentrations.
sections.clinical_effects: INR can rise substantially within just a few days of starting fluconazole, with increased risk of bruising, mucosal bleeding, and major hemorrhage if the dose is not adjusted promptly.
sections.management: If fluconazole cannot be avoided, preemptively reduce the warfarin dose (often by 25-50%) and monitor INR closely; an alternative antifungal with less CYP2C9 inhibition may be preferable when appropriate.
sections.monitoring: Check INR within 3-5 days of starting fluconazole and again after it is stopped, continuing frequent checks until the INR is stable at the adjusted dose.

evidence.citation: Diflucan (fluconazole) FDA prescribing information, Drug Interactions section
